mnemonics.title: Two Keys = Asymmetric
mnemonics.content: Public key cryptography uses TWO mathematically related keys: Public (for encryption/verification) and Private (for decryption/signing). Remember: Asymmetric = Different keys. One key encrypts, the other decrypts. This solves the key distribution problem!

mnemonics.title: RSA Key Generation: P-Q-N-Phi-E-D
mnemonics.content: RSA key generation follows P-Q-N-Phi-E-D: Pick two Primes (p, q) → Calculate N = p×q → Calculate Phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) → choose E (public exponent, coprime to phi) → compute D (private exponent, inverse of e mod phi). Remember this order!

mnemonics.title: C = M^e, M = C^d (mod n)
mnemonics.content: RSA formulas: Encryption: C = M^e mod n (use public key e). Decryption: M = C^d mod n (use private key d). The 'e' in encryption stands for 'exposed' (public), 'd' in decryption for 'dark secret' (private). Both use the same modulus n.

mnemonics.title: Security = Integer Factorization Problem
mnemonics.content: RSA security relies on: multiplying two primes is EASY (p×q = n), but factoring n back into p and q is HARD. This is a one-way function with a trapdoor. The trapdoor? Knowing p or q. Without it, breaking RSA requires factoring huge numbers.

mnemonics.title: Hybrid = RSA + AES
mnemonics.content: Real systems use HYBRID encryption: RSA is slow but solves key exchange; AES is fast but needs shared keys. Solution: Use RSA to securely exchange an AES session key, then use AES for bulk data. Best of both worlds! This is how HTTPS works.

keyPoints: Public key cryptography uses a mathematically related key pair: public key (shareable) and private key (secret).
RSA solves the key distribution problem inherent in symmetric cryptography.
RSA security is based on the computational difficulty of factoring large composite numbers into prime factors.
Key generation involves selecting two large primes, computing n and φ(n), choosing e, and finding d as the modular inverse of e.
RSA can be used for both encryption (C = M^e mod n) and digital signatures (reverse process with private key).
RSA is much slower than symmetric algorithms, so hybrid systems combine RSA (for key exchange) with AES (for data encryption).
Modern RSA implementations require padding schemes (OAEP, PSS) and adequate key sizes (2048+ bits minimum).
commonMistakes: Confusing which key to use: public key encrypts/verifies, private key decrypts/signs.
Forgetting that d and e are modular multiplicative inverses modulo φ(n), not modulo n.
Believing RSA can replace symmetric encryption for all purposes; it's too slow for bulk data.
Using small key sizes (512, 1024 bits) which are now considered insecure; minimum 2048 bits.
Thinking public key cryptography is always slower; it's only the asymmetric operations that are slow.
Computing φ(n) incorrectly as (p-1)×(q-1) requires knowing the prime factors p and q.
Forgetting that RSA without proper padding (raw RSA) is vulnerable to various attacks.
